howItWorks.intro: This password strength calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.

rules.name: Entropy
rules.explanation: Bits of entropy based on character pool and password length.

howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates based on standard formulas. Verify with current local data for your specific situation.

faq.question: What makes a strong password?
faq.answer: 12+ characters with mixed case, numbers, and symbols. Length matters most.

faq.question: How fast can passwords be cracked?
faq.answer: Modern GPUs can try 10 billion combinations per second against common hashes.

faq.question: Is the Password Strength Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard practical calculation formulas trusted by users. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

faq.question: What password length should I enter?
faq.answer: Enter the most accurate password length value you have available. If you're estimating, use a conservative figure. You can always run the calculator again with different values to see how changes affect the results.

sections.title: What Is the Password Strength Calculator?
sections.body: The Password Strength Calculator is a free online tool designed for users who need quick, accurate calculations in the practical calculation space. By entering your password length, uppercase letters, lowercase letters, you get instant results including entropy, brute force crack time, strength. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting entropy right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In practical calculation, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by users worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to get accurate results with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your password length and need to find the right entropy. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Password Length
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your password length — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: uppercase letters, lowercase letters, numbers, symbols. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your entropy, brute force crack time, strength. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your password length before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both entropy and brute force crack time — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to get accurate results.
If you're unsure about your symbols, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
